lineitem.notes: Extremely hot - touched by all 3 queries. Date filters highly selective (1-12 months out of ~7 years = 1.4-14% selectivity). Ideal candidate for columnar storage with date-based zone maps.

orders.notes: Only accessed in Q3. Date filter on o_orderdate is selective. PK-FK join with customer and lineitem.

customer.notes: Only in Q3. Very selective filter on c_mktsegment (1 value out of 5 = 20% selectivity). Should be build side of join.

nation.notes: Not used in sample queries, but tiny dimension - always fits in L1 cache.

region.notes: Not used in sample queries, but tiny dimension.

partsupp.notes: Many-to-many relationship between part and supplier. Not accessed in sample queries.

joins.notes: Q3 join. Build hash table on filtered customer (20% selectivity on mktsegment = ~30K rows), probe with orders. FK join means no duplicates on orders side.

Q3 join. Orders is filtered by date (<1995-03-15), lineitem filtered by date (>1995-03-15). Non-overlapping date ranges mean both sides are selective. FK join, but 1:N relationship (avg 4 lineitems per order).

filters.notes: Keeps ~98% of data (1998-12-01 minus 90 days = 1998-09-02, covers most of the ~7 year TPC-H date range). Low filtering power but enables partition elimination.
1-year range (1994-01-01 to 1995-01-01) out of ~7 years = 14% selectivity. Very selective - excellent candidate for zone maps or sorted index.
Range 0.05-0.07 (2% wide) out of typical 0-0.10 range = ~20% selectivity. Applied after date filter.
Quantity < 24. Typical range 1-50, so ~48% selectivity. Least selective filter in Q6.
'BUILDING' is 1 of 5 market segments = 20% selectivity. Should be applied before join to reduce customer table from ~150K to ~30K rows.
Before 1995-03-15, covers ~3.2 years of ~7 year range = 46% selectivity.
After 1995-03-15, covers ~3.8 years = 54% selectivity. Complementary to orders date filter - non-overlapping ranges reduce join intermediate size.

aggregations.notes: Perfect hash aggregation opportunity - both columns are single chars with ~3 distinct values each. Max 9 groups, likely 4 active (combinations like 'A'/'F', 'O'). In-register aggregation possible. Pre-aggregation opportunity if lineitem is sorted/partitioned by these columns.

High-cardinality grouping (l_orderkey is effectively unique after filters). Hash aggregation with ~100K-500K groups expected after filters. LIMIT 10 with ORDER BY means top-K optimization possible (min-heap of size 10 instead of full hash table).

ordering.notes: Sort on 4-6 groups after aggregation - trivial cost. Could even use insertion sort.
ORDER BY + LIMIT 10 = top-K optimization. Don't need full sort - use min-heap or partial quicksort. Revenue is computed aggregate, not base column.

column_co_access_groups.notes: Store these column groups together for cache locality. l_extendedprice and l_discount are accessed together in all 3 queries - prime candidates for adjacent column placement.

optimization_opportunities: Columnar storage for lineitem: All queries project only 4-7 columns from 16 total. Columnar layout reduces I/O by 50-75%.
Zone maps on l_shipdate: All 3 queries filter on date. Zone maps enable chunk skipping - could eliminate 50-90% of data blocks without reading them.
Perfect hash aggregation for Q1: Only 4-6 groups (l_returnflag, l_linestatus). Use direct indexing instead of hash table - saves 90% of aggregation overhead.
Top-K optimization for Q3: LIMIT 10 with ORDER BY. Maintain min-heap of 10 elements during aggregation instead of sorting full result set. 10-100x faster for large intermediates.
Filter pushdown in Q3: Apply c_mktsegment='BUILDING' before customer-orders join. Reduces customer from 150K to 30K rows (5x reduction in join cost).
Predicate pushdown in Q3: Date filters on orders and lineitem can be pushed to scans, reducing join input sizes by ~50% each.
Early projection: Q6 only needs 4 columns from lineitem. Project before filters to improve cache locality and SIMD efficiency.
Vectorized execution for Q1 and Q6: Sequential scans with arithmetic and aggregation - ideal for 2048-tuple vectors. Expect 5-10x speedup vs tuple-at-a-time.
Hash join optimization for Q3: Build on smaller side (customer after filter ~30K rows), probe with orders (~690K rows after date filter). Linear probing hash table fits in L3 cache (2-3 MB).
SIMD for Q6 filters: Four predicates on numeric columns (shipdate, discount, quantity) can be evaluated with AVX2 - process 4-8 values per instruction.
Bloom filter for Q3 joins: Create bloom filter on customer.c_custkey after mktsegment filter. Probe during orders scan eliminates ~80% of orders before hash join.

Correlated filter exploitation in Q3: o_orderdate < '1995-03-15' AND l_shipdate > '1995-03-15' creates non-overlapping time ranges. Orders and lineitems can be pre-filtered independently with high selectivity.
Late materialization for Q1: Filter on l_shipdate first (returns row IDs), then fetch other columns only for qualifying rows. Reduces memory bandwidth by 98%.
Dictionary encoding for l_returnflag and l_linestatus: Both are CHAR(1) with 3 distinct values. Encode as 2-bit integers (4x compression, faster comparisons).
Pre-aggregation/partial aggregation for Q1: If data is sorted/partitioned by (returnflag, linestatus), can aggregate within blocks and combine. Reduces hash table pressure.

Join ordering for Q3: customer ⋈ orders ⋈ lineitem. Optimal order: (customer ⋈ orders) ⋈ lineitem. Customer is smallest after filter, builds 30K-row hash table. Intermediate is ~200K orders, joins with ~800K lineitem rows.
Multi-predicate filter ordering in Q6: Evaluate cheapest/most selective first. Order: l_shipdate (14% selective), l_discount (20%), l_quantity (48%). Early-out reduces work by 86%.
Parallel execution: Q1 and Q6 are embarrassingly parallel (scan + aggregate). Partition lineitem by ranges, aggregate per thread, combine. Linear scaling to 8-16 cores.
Expression reuse in Q1: l_extendedprice * (1 - l_discount) computed twice. Calculate once, reuse for both sum_disc_price and sum_charge. 50% reduction in arithmetic.

Sorted index on l_shipdate: All queries filter on date ranges. B-tree or sorted blocks enable range scans instead of full table scans. Combined with columnar storage: read only qualifying date ranges from all columns.

critical_path_analysis.reasoning: Q3 is the most complex: 3-way join with filters on all tables, high-cardinality grouping, and top-K sort. Q1 and Q6 are simple scans. Q3 likely 10-100x slower than Q1/Q6.
critical_path_analysis.q1_complexity: Low - single table scan with 4-6 group aggregation. ~5-10M rows scanned, 6 aggregates updated per row. Dominated by scan I/O (~200-400ms for SF=1).
critical_path_analysis.q3_complexity: High - 3-way join produces large intermediate. Customer (30K after filter) ⋈ orders (690K after filter) = ~200K rows. Joined with lineitem (3.2M after filter) = ~800K-1M rows. Hash aggregation on ~100K-500K groups. Top-10 sort. Dominated by join and aggregation (~2-5 seconds for SF=1 without optimization).
critical_path_analysis.q6_complexity: Low - single table scan with 4 filters, single aggregate. ~840K rows scanned (14% of 6M), simple SUM. Dominated by scan I/O (~50-100ms for SF=1).
critical_path_analysis.optimization_priority: Focus on Q3 join ordering, filter pushdown, and top-K optimization. Q1 benefits from perfect hashing and vectorization. Q6 benefits from zone maps and SIMD filters.

workload_characteristics.dominant_pattern: Analytical scans with date-based filtering and aggregation
workload_characteristics.data_skew: Date ranges are selective (14-54%), creating temporal skew. Market segment filter creates value skew (20% selectivity).
workload_characteristics.join_graph: Q3 forms a chain: customer → orders → lineitem. All PK-FK joins.
workload_characteristics.memory_footprint: Q1: ~200KB hash table. Q3: ~10-20MB hash tables + intermediates. Q6: ~100KB aggregate state. All fit in L3 cache (8-32MB typical).
workload_characteristics.parallelism_potential: High - all queries are data-parallel. Q1 and Q6 have no dependencies. Q3 can pipeline customer⋈orders, then parallel probe with lineitem partitions.
workload_characteristics.compression_opportunity: High - columnar storage with dictionary encoding (returnflag, linestatus, mktsegment), lightweight integer compression (quantity, dates), and delta encoding (prices) could achieve 5-10x compression.
workload_characteristics.cache_behavior: Sequential scans benefit from prefetching. Hash joins have random access pattern but tables fit in L3. Late materialization improves cache hit rate by 50-80%.

summary: TPC-H workload is dominated by large sequential scans of the lineitem fact table (6M rows) with highly selective date filters (14-98% of data). All 3 queries benefit from columnar storage, vectorized execution, and zone maps on l_shipdate. Q1 is a simple scan-aggregate with trivial grouping (4-6 groups) - perfect hash aggregation is optimal. Q6 is a filtered scan with single aggregate - SIMD filter evaluation and early projection are key. Q3 is the critical path: 3-way join with filter pushdown opportunities, top-K optimization for LIMIT 10, and optimal join ordering (customer → orders → lineitem). The workload exhibits strong column co-access patterns (l_extendedprice + l_discount always together) and temporal filtering patterns (date ranges). Optimization priorities: (1) columnar storage + zone maps for I/O reduction, (2) vectorized execution for CPU efficiency, (3) Q3 join optimization (filter pushdown, build side selection, top-K), (4) perfect hashing for Q1, (5) SIMD filters for Q6. Expected speedup from full optimization: 10-50x over naive tuple-at-a-time row-store execution.
